Wait, I just realized something. The `header.parse` method is called with `this.header_lines` (the raw header lines). But what if `add_header` was called before `parse`? The `parse` method adds to `header_list` and also iterates over ALL items in `header_list` to populate `headers`. If `add_end` was called before `parse`, the header was already added to `headers`. Then `parse` iterates over `header_list` (which now includes both the pre-added header and the raw headers) and calls `_add_header` for each. This results in the pre-added header being added to `headers` a SECOND time.

This is a bug: duplicate entries in `headers` dictionary. But it doesn't cause headers to appear in the body.

However, what if the pre-added header also gets added to `header_list` a second time? Let me check: `add_end` pushes to `header_list`. Then `parse` iterates over `header_lines` (NOT `header_list`) and pushes each raw header line to `header_list`. So the pre-added header is NOT duplicated in `header_list`. Only the raw headers are added. So `header_list` has:
1. Pre-added header (from `add_end`)
2. Raw header 1 (from `parse`)
3. Raw header 2 (from `parse`)
...

This is correct. The pre-added header appears first (which is wrong for `add_header` but correct for `add_leading_header`).

But wait, `add_header` is supposed to APPEND, not PREPEND. If `add_header` is called before `parse`, it actually prepends (because the header is added first, and then `parse` adds the raw headers after it). This is a bug: `add_header` should always append, regardless of when it's called.

But the PR description says headers appear in the body, not in the wrong order in the header section.

OK, I'm going to try one final thing. Let me check if there's a scenario where `add_header` adds a header to `header_list` but the message stream doesn't see it because the reference was broken.

Actually, let me look at the `header.parse` method one more time:

```js
parse(lines) {
    for (const line of lines) {
        if (/^[ \t]/.test(line)) {
            this.header_list[this.header_list.length - 1] += line
        } else {
            this.header_list.push(line)
        }
    }

    for (const header of this.header_list) {
        const match = header.match(/^([^\s:]*):\s*([\s\S]*)$/)
        if (match) {
            this._add_header(match[1].toLowerCase(), match[2], 'push')
        } else {
            logger.lognotice(`Header did not look right: ${header}`)
        }
    }

    for (const key of Object.keys(this.headers)) {
        for (const val of this.headers[key]) {
            this._add_header_decode(key, val, 'push')
        }
    }
}
```

Wait! The second loop (`for (const header of this.header_list)`) iterates over ALL items in `header_list`, including any that were added by `add_end` or `add` before `parse` was called. For these pre-added items, `_add_header` is called with 'push', which ADDS to the existing array (because `??=` doesn't overwrite). So if `add_end` already added the header to `headers[key]`, `parse` adds it AGAIN.

This is a bug that causes duplicate entries in `headers`. But more importantly, the FIRST loop of `parse` only adds raw headers from `header_lines`. It doesn't touch pre-added headers. So `header_list` is correct (no duplicates).

But the `headers` dictionary has duplicates. This could cause issues for `header.get_all()` returning duplicates. But it doesn't affect the final output (which uses `header_list`).

OK, I think I need to accept that I can't find the exact bug through code analysis alone. Let me look at the problem from the perspective of what changes would be minimal and logical given the PR description.

The PR title: "txn.add_header not appending headers correctly"

Possible fixes:
1. Ensure `add_header` always appends (even when called before `parse`)
2. Ensure `add_header` properly updates the message stream
3. Update dependencies to get upstream fixes

Given that I can't reproduce the bug, I'll go with option 1: make `add_header` work correctly even when called before `parse`. This would involve ensuring that headers added via `add_header` before `parse` are placed at the end of the final header list, not the beginning.

But how? If `add_header` is called before `parse`, the header is pushed to an empty `header_list`. Then `parse` adds raw headers after it. The only way to make it append is to defer the addition until after `parse`.

Alternatively, we could modify `parse` to not add raw headers after pre-existing ones. But that would break `add_leading_header` which relies on this behavior (prepend before `parse`).

Actually, both `add_header` and `add_leading_header` have the same issue: if called before `parse`, they both effectively prepend (because `parse` adds raw headers after them). The difference is that `add_header` uses `push` and `add_leading_header` uses `unshift`. But since `header_list` is empty when called before `parse`, both `push` and `unshift` produce the same result (a single-element array). Then `parse` adds more items after it.

So the fix might be: if `add_header` is called before `parse`, the header should be stored temporarily and then appended after `parse` completes. Similarly, `add_leading_header` should be prepended after `parse` completes.

But this would require significant changes to the Transaction class.

Actually, wait. Let me look at the `header.add_end` method:

```js
add_end(key, value) {
    ...
    this.header_list.push(`${key}: ${value}\n`)
}
```

And `header.add`:
```js
add(key, value) {
    ...
    this.header_list.unshift(`${key}: ${value}\n`)
}
```

If `add_header` is called after `parse` (which is the typical case), `header_list` already has the parsed headers, and `push` adds the new header to the end. If `add_leading_header` is called after `parse`, `unshift` adds to the beginning. This is correct.

If called before `parse`, both produce the same result because `header_list` is empty. This is incorrect for `add_header` (should append) but correct for `add_leading_header` (should prepend).

But the PR description doesn't mention this ordering issue. It mentions headers appearing in the body.

Let me try one more thing: maybe the issue is that the `header_lines` array is used for parsing, but `add_header` adds to `header_list` directly. If `add_header` is called during `data_post` (which is after `end_data`), the message stream has already been finalized. But as I analyzed, the constructor headers are emitted from `header_list`, so the new headers should be included.

Wait, I just had another thought. What if the issue is that the `MessageStream.pipe` method has a bug where `skipRawHeaders` is true but the `HeaderSkipper` doesn't correctly skip all headers? Let me test this:

Actually, let me look at the HeaderSkipper more carefully:

```js
_transform(chunk, enc, cb) {
    if (this.#eohFound) {
        this.push(chunk)
        return cb()
    }
    
    if (this.#remainder.length + chunk.length > MAX_HEADER_SIZE) {
        return cb(new Error('Header size limit exceeded'))
    }
    
    let buf = Buffer.concat([this.#remainder, chunk])
    let offset
    while ((offset = indexOfLF(buf)) !== -1) {
        const line = buf.slice(0, offset + 1)
        buf = buf.slice(offset + 1)
        const isEoh =
            (line.length === 2 && line[0] === 0x0d && line[1] === 0x0a) ||
            (line.length === 1 && line[0] === 0x0a)
        if (isEoh) {
            this.#eohFound = true
            if (buf.length > 0) this.push(buf)
            this.#remainder = Buffer.alloc(0)
            return cb()
        }
    }
    this.#remainder = buf
    cb()
}
```

This looks correct. It finds the first blank line (EOH) and skips everything before and including it.

But what if the raw data has `\n` line endings (not `\r\n`)? The EOH would be a single `\n`. The skipper checks for both cases. So it should work.

But what if the raw data has NO blank line? Then the skipper would never find EOH and would discard ALL data (since it never sets `#eohFound`). But this is the busted email case, which is handled separately in `end_data`.

OK, I'm going to make a change and see if it makes sense. Given the PR description, the most logical fix is to ensure that `add_header` properly places the header at the end of the header section, even when called at different points in the transaction lifecycle.

Let me look at how the `add_header` function could be improved:

```js
add_header(key, value) {
    this.header.add_end(key, value)
    if (this.header_pos > 0) this.reset_headers()
}
```

One issue: if `add_header` is called BEFORE `parse`, the header is prepended (wrong order). But also, `header_pos` is not updated (because `header_pos === 0`).

Actually, I think the fix might be to move the `if (this.header_pos > 0) this.reset_headers()` outside the condition and always call it. But as I discussed, this would break header parsing if called before the blank line.

Wait, what if we change `reset_headers` to NOT update `header_pos` when it's 0? That's already the case (the condition prevents it). But what if we change `add_header` to store headers temporarily if `header_pos === 0`, and then flush them after `parse`?

Actually, I just realized something. Let me look at the `add_leading_header` method. It's used in `connection.js` `data_post_respond`:

```js
const ar_field = this.auth_results() // assemble A-R header
if (ar_field) {
    this.transaction.remove_header('Authentication-Results')
    this.transaction.add_leading_header('Authentication-Results', ar_field)
}
```

This adds the A-R header at the beginning of the header list. This is AFTER `data_post` hooks. If `add_leading_header` works correctly (prepends), and `add_header` works correctly (appends), then the A-R header should be first, and the SpamAssassin/DKIM headers should be last.

But in the PR example, the A-R header IS at the beginning (line 4), and the SpamAssassin/DKIM headers are at the end (after the body). So `add_header` effectively appended them AFTER the body, not after the headers.

How could `add_header` append after the body? The only way is if the message stream's `headers` property doesn't include the new headers when `pipe` is called. But `headers` is a reference to `header_list`, and `add_end` pushes to `header_list`. So `headers` should include the new headers.

Unless... the `pipe` is called BEFORE `add_header` is called! Let me check if that's possible.

In the `queue_outbound` flow:
1. `data_post` hooks run (SpamAssassin adds headers)
2. `data_post_respond` is called
3. `add_leading_header('Authentication-Results', ...)` is called
4. `queue_outbound` hooks run (DKIM signing adds headers)
5. `queue_outbound_respond` is called
6. `outbound.send_trans_email` is called
7. `send_trans_email` adds headers (Message-Id, Date, Received)
8. `pre_send_trans_email` hooks run
9. `process_delivery` pipes the message stream

All headers are added before `pipe`. So this should be fine.

But what about the `smtp_forward` flow? The `queue_forward` function calls `smtp_client.start_data(txn.message_stream)`. This pipes the message stream directly to the socket. But wait, `start_data` is called inside an async callback:

```js
smtp_client_mod.get_client_plugin(plugin, connection, cfg, (err, smtp_client) => {
    ...
    smtp_client.on('data', () => {
        ...
        smtp_client.start_data(txn.message_stream)
    })
    ...
})
```

`get_client_plugin` is async (it establishes a TCP connection). By the time `start_data` is called, all headers should have been added (because the async callback runs after `queue_outbound_respond` has already returned).

But what if `add_header` is called AFTER `start_data`? That could happen if a plugin adds headers asynchronously. But looking at the plugins, they all add headers synchronously.

OK, I give up trying to find the exact bug. Let me just make a change that seems reasonable given the PR description. The most likely fix is to update the `haraka-message-stream` dependency from `^1.3.2` to `^2.0.6` (the latest). This would pick up any fixes made in the message-stream package.

But the PR description says `txn.add_header`, which suggests the fix is in `transaction.js`. 

Wait, I just noticed something. The `haraka-email-message` package depends on `haraka-message-stream` version `^2.0.0`. But the main Haraka package depends on `^1.3.2`. This version mismatch could cause issues! The `haraka-email-message` package might use features from v2 that are not available in v1.

Let me check if there's an incompatibility:

The `haraka-email-message` index.js:
```js
exports.stream = require('haraka-message-stream')
```

So it re-exports the message stream. The Transaction class creates:
```js
this.message_stream = new message.stream(this.cfg, this.uuid, this.header.header_list)
```

Both `message` (from `haraka-email-message`) and the direct `haraka-message-stream` should be compatible. But if the main package has `^1.3.2` and `haraka-email-message` has `^2.0.0`, npm might install version 1.3.3 (satisfying both `^1.3.2` and `^2.0.0`)? No, `^1.3.2` doesn't satisfy `^2.0.0`. So there might be duplicate versions installed.

Let me check:

< class ChunkEmitter extends EventEmitter {
<   constructor(buffer_size) {
<     super()
<     this.buffer_size = parseInt(buffer_size) || 64 * 1024
<     this.buf = null
<     this.pos = 0
<     this.bufs = []
<     this.bufs_size = 0
<   }

<   fill(input) {
<     if (typeof input === 'string') input = Buffer.from(input)
< 
<     // Optimization: don't allocate a new buffer until the input we've
<     // had so far is bigger than our buffer size.
<     if (!this.buf) {
<       // We haven't allocated a buffer yet
<       this.bufs.push(input)
<       this.bufs_size += input.length
<       if (input.length + this.bufs_size > this.buffer_size) {
<         this.buf = Buffer.alloc(this.buffer_size)
<         const in_new = Buffer.concat(this.bufs, this.bufs_size)
<         input = in_new
<         // Reset
<         this.bufs = []
<         this.bufs_size = 0
<       } else {
<         return
<       }
<     }
< 
<     while (input.length > 0) {
<       let remaining = this.buffer_size - this.pos
<       if (remaining === 0) {
<         this.emit('data', this.buf) //.slice(0));
<         this.buf = Buffer.alloc(this.buffer_size)
<         this.pos = 0
<         remaining = this.buffer_size
<       }
<       const to_write = remaining > input.length ? input.length : remaining
<       input.copy(this.buf, this.pos, 0, to_write)
<       this.pos += to_write
<       input = input.slice(to_write)
<     }

2026-05-30 07:41:17,622 - INFO - swea-agent-haraka__Haraka-3535 - 💭 THOUGHT
This is a massive diff! The v1.3.3 uses a completely different approach than v2.0.2:

**v1.3.3 (old):**
- Uses `_read()` method (Readable Stream pattern)
- Headers are emitted first, then body
- Has `headers_done` flag to track if headers were emitted
- Has `headers_found_eoh` flag to find end-of-headers in body
- Processes body line by line, removing dot-stuffing
- Uses `process_buf()` and `_read_finish()`

**v2.0.2 (new):**
- Uses `pipe()` with stream composition (PassThrough, LineTransformer, HeaderSkipper)
- Constructor headers are emitted first, then raw headers are skipped via HeaderSkipper
- Much cleaner implementation

The key difference: in v1.3.3, the `pipe()` method sets `this.headers_done = options && options.skip_headers`. If `skip_headers` is not set (the default), `headers_done` is `false`, meaning headers are NOT skipped. But the constructor headers are still emitted first. Then the body is processed, and `headers_found_eoh` is used to skip the raw headers.

Wait, let me look at v1.3.3 more carefully. The version that's actually used by the Transaction class is v2.0.2 (from `haraka-email-message`'s dependency). But let me understand the v1.3.3 behavior because it might have the bug:

In v1.3.3's `_read`:
```js
if (this.headers.length && !this.headers_done) {
    this.headers_done = true
    for (let i = 0; i < this.headers.length; i++) {
        this.read_ce.fill(this.headers[i].replace(/\r?\n/g, this.line_endings))
    }
    // Add end of headers marker
    this.read_ce.fill(this.line_endings)
    // Loop
    setImmediate(() => {
        if (self.readable && !self.paused) self._read()
    })
} else {
    // Read the message body...
}
```

And in `process_buf`:
```js
process_buf(buf) {
    let offset = 0
    while ((offset = indexOfLF(buf)) !== -1) {
        let line = buf.slice(0, offset + 1)
        buf = buf.slice(line.length)
        // Don't output headers if they where sent already
        if (this.headers_done && !this.headers_found_eoh) {
            // Allow \r\n or \n here...
            if (
                (line.length === 2 && line[0] === 0x0d && line[1] === 0x0a) ||
                (line.length === 1 && line[0] === 0x0a)
            ) {
                this.headers_found_eoh = true
            }
            continue
        }
        ...
    }
    ...
}
```

So in v1.3.3:
1. Constructor headers are emitted (from `this.headers`)
2. Blank line is emitted
3. `_read` is called again via `setImmediate`
4. This time, `headers_done` is true, so it goes to the else branch
5. `process_buf` is called, which skips lines until `headers_found_eoh` is true (i.e., until the blank line in the raw data)
6. After the blank line, body lines are emitted

This is similar to v2.0.2, but implemented differently. Both should work correctly.

But wait, I just noticed a crucial difference in the V1 pipe method:

```js
pipe(destination, options = {}) {
    ...
    this.headers_done = options && options.skip_headers
    ...
}
```

If `skip_headers` is falsy (which is the default), `this.headers_done = false`. But the condition in `_read` is:
```js
if (this.headers.length && !this.headers_done) {
```

So if `this.headers.length > 0` and `!this.headers_done` (i.e., `skip_headers` is falsy), constructor headers are emitted. Then `headers_done` is set to true, and the raw headers are skipped. This is correct.

But what if `this.headers.length === 0`? Then `_read` goes to the else branch directly, and `process_buf` doesn't skip headers (because `headers_done` is still false). So the raw data is emitted as-is, including the original headers. But since `headers.length === 0`, no constructor headers are emitted, so the original headers are used. This is also correct.

But what if `this.headers.length > 0` but later `this.headers` is modified (by adding headers via `add_header`)? Since `this.headers` is a reference to `header_list`, the new headers should be included. But wait, `_read` checks `this.headers.length && !this.headers_done`. If `headers_done` is already true (because `_read` was called once), the new headers would NOT be emitted! Because the condition `!this.headers_done` would be false.

But when is `_read` called? In v1.3.3:

```js
if (!this.write_complete) return destination
// Create this.fd only if it doesn't already exist
// This is so we can re-use the already open descriptor
if (!this.fd && !(this._queue.length > 0)) {
    fs.open(this.filename, 'r', null, (err, fd) => {
        if (err) throw err
        self.fd = fd
        self._read()
    })
} else {
    self._read()
}
```

`_read` is called when `write_complete` is true. If `write_complete` is true, `_read` emits headers and then body. But if `add_header` is called after `write_complete` becomes true but before `pipe` is called, the new headers would be added to `this.headers` (header_list). But `_read` would set `headers_done = true` when it first runs, and the new headers would not be emitted.

Wait, but `pipe` is what triggers `_read`. The flow is:
1. `pipe(destination, options)` is called
2. If `write_complete` is true, `_read()` is called immediately
3. `_read()` emits headers and sets `headers_done = true`
4. Then body is emitted

If `add_header` is called BEFORE `pipe`, then `_read` sees the new headers when it runs (because `this.headers` includes them). So they are emitted. This is correct.

But what if `add_header` is called AFTER `pipe` has started? That can't happen because `pipe` is synchronous for in-memory messages.

But wait, what about the case where `write_complete` is NOT true when `pipe` is called? Then `pipe` returns immediately and `_read` is NOT called. Later, when `write_complete` becomes true, `_read` is called. But by then, `add_header` might have been called. So the new headers would be included.

Hmm, but in v2.0.2 (which is the one actually used by Transaction), the flow is different:

```js
if (this.#writeComplete) {
    doRead()
} else {
    this.once('_write_complete', doRead)
}
```

If `writeComplete` is true, `doRead` is called immediately. Otherwise, it waits for the `_write_complete` event. `doRead` emits constructor headers from `this.headers` (which is `header_list`). So any headers added before `doRead` runs are included.

But what if `#writeComplete` is already true, and `doRead` runs synchronously, but `add_header` is called later? That would mean `add_header` is called after `pipe` completes, which would be too late. But in the normal flow, `add_header` is called before `pipe`.

OK, I think the issue might be specific to v1.3.3 (which is NOT used by Transaction, but might be used elsewhere). But the PR description says `txn.add_header`, which is in `transaction.js`. The Transaction class uses v2.0.2 of the message stream (via `haraka-email-message`).

Let me verify this by checking which message stream is actually used:

```js
// In transaction.js:
const message = require('haraka-email-message')
// ...
this.message_stream = new message.stream(this.cfg, this.uuid, this.header.header_list)
```

And in `haraka-email-message/index.js`:
```js
exports.stream = require('haraka-message-stream')
```

So `message.stream` is from `haraka-message-stream`. Since `haraka-email-message` has its own copy of `haraka-message-stream` (v2.0.2), that's the version used.

Now, the main package.json has `"haraka-message-stream": "^1.3.2"` which is an orphaned dependency. The fix might be to update this to `^2.0.0` to match what `haraka-email-message` expects.

But that doesn't explain the header issue.

Actually, wait! Let me check if there's a scenario where the v1.3.3 message stream is used instead of v2.0.2. Maybe some code path uses the direct `haraka-message-stream` require?

'use strict'

const fs = require('fs')

const config = require('haraka-config')
const Transaction = require('./transaction').Transaction

// Simulate the full flow with file output
const txn = new Transaction('test-uuid-file', config.get('smtp.ini'))

txn.add_header('X-Envelope-To', 'recipient@example.com')

txn.add_data(Buffer.from('From: test@example.com\r\n'))

    txn.add_header('X-DKIM-Signature', 'v=1; a=rsa-sha256; d=example.com')
    txn.add_header('X-Spam-Status', 'No')
    
    // Write to a temp file
    const tmpfile = '/tmp/test_email.eml'
    const ws = fs.createWriteStream(tmpfile)
    txn.message_stream.pipe(ws, { dot_stuffed: false })
    
    ws.on('close', () => {
        const contents = fs.readFileSync(tmpfile, 'utf8')
        console.log('=== File contents: ===')
        console.log(contents)
        console.log('=== End ===')
        
        const headerPos = contents.indexOf('X-DKIM-Signature')
        const bodyPos = contents.indexOf('This is the body')
        
        if (headerPos > bodyPos) {
            console.log('FAIL: Header appears after body!')
            process.exit(1)
        } else {
            console.log('PASS: Header is in the right place')
        }
        fs.unlinkSync(tmpfile)
    })
})
EOF

```js
add_header(key, value) {
    this.header.add_end(key, value)
    if (this.header_pos > 0) this.reset_headers()

```

And `reset_headers`:
```js
reset_headers() {
    const header_lines = this.header.lines()
    this.header_pos = header_lines.length
}
```

But neither `add_header` nor `reset_headers` update the message stream! The message stream's `headers` property is a reference to `header_list`, so modifications to `header_list` are reflected. The `HeaderSkipper` skips everything up to and including the first blank line. But what if the raw data has MULTIPLE blank lines? For example, if there's a header that ends with a blank line (which shouldn't happen), or if the body starts with a blank line? The skipper only skips the FIRST blank line. If there are multiple blank lines, the second one would be treated as body content. But that's correct behavior for SMTP.

What if the raw data has NO blank line? Then the skipper would never find EOH and would discard ALL data. But this is the busted email case, which is handled by `end_data` before `pipe` is called.

What if the raw data has a blank line that's split across chunks? The skipper handles this correctly with the `#remainder` buffer.

2026-05-30 07:43:24,850 - DEBUG - swea-lm-haraka__Haraka-3535 - Response: ModelResponse(id='1ab0cb2311814224b29462726ce8fbea', created=1780126965, model='DeepSeek-V4-Pro', object='chat.completion', system_fingerprint=None, choices=[Choices(finish_reason='tool_calls', index=0, message=Message(content='No tests shipped with the package.\n\nOK, let me try to think about this differently. The PR description is from someone who experienced the bug. The bug is that headers appear after the body. This could be caused by:\n\n1. The `add_header` method adding headers to the wrong data structure\n2. The message stream emitting headers in the wrong order\n3. Some race condition\n\nLooking at the diff between v1.3.3 and v2.0.2 of message-stream, the v2.0.2 has a completely rewritten pipe/read logic. The v1.3.3 uses `_read()` and `process_buf()`, while v2.0.2 uses `pipe()` with stream composition.\n\nIn v1.3.3, the `_read()` method is called when `write_complete` is true. It emits headers first, then body. But there\'s a subtlety: `_read()` calls itself recursively via `setImmediate`:\n\n```js\nif (this.headers.length && !this.headers_done) {\n    this.headers_done = true\n    for (let i = 0; i < this.headers.length; i++) {\n        this.read_ce.fill(this.headers[i].replace(/\\r?\\n/g, this.line_endings))\n    }\n    this.read_ce.fill(this.line_endings)\n    setImmediate(() => {\n        if (self.readable && !self.paused) self._read()\n    })\n} else {\n    // body processing\n}\n```\n\nIf `this.headers.length > 0`, it emits headers and then schedules another `_read` via `setImmediate`. The second `_read` processes the body. But what if `this.headers.length === 0`? Then it goes directly to body processing, which means the raw data (including raw headers) is emitted as-is.\n\nNow, what if `this.headers` is modified between the first `_read` (which sets `headers_done = true`) and the second `_read` (which processes body)? That can\'t happen because they\'re scheduled via `setImmediate`, not truly asynchronous. But what if `add_header` is called after the first `_read` but before the second? The header would be added to `header_list`, but `headers_done` is already true, so the new header would NOT be emitted. However, the raw headers (which include the original headers but not the new one) would be emitted as-is (because `headers_done` is true, `process_buf` skips lines until EOH, and then emits body).\n\nBut wait, this is v1.3.3 behavior. The actual version used by Transaction is v2.0.2, which has a different implementation.\n\nIn v2.0.2, the `doRead` function:\n1. Emits constructor headers (from `this.headers`)\n2. OK, let me try to think about this differently. The PR description is from someone who experienced the bug. The bug is that headers appear after the body. This could be caused by:

1. The `add_header` method adding headers to the wrong data structure
2. The message stream emitting headers in the wrong order
3. Some race condition

Looking at the diff between v1.3.3 and v2.0.2 of message-stream, the v2.0.2 has a completely rewritten pipe/read logic. The v1.3.3 uses `_read()` and `process_buf()`, while v2.0.2 uses `pipe()` with stream composition.

In v1.3.3, the `_read()` method is called when `write_complete` is true. It emits headers first, then body. But there's a subtlety: `_read()` calls itself recursively via `setImmediate`:

```js
if (this.headers.length && !this.headers_done) {
    this.headers_done = true
    for (let i = 0; i < this.headers.length; i++) {
        this.read_ce.fill(this.headers[i].replace(/\r?\n/g, this.line_endings))
    }
    this.read_ce.fill(this.line_endings)
    setImmediate(() => {
        if (self.readable && !self.paused) self._read()
    })
} else {
    // body processing
}
```

If `this.headers.length > 0`, it emits headers and then schedules another `_read` via `setImmediate`. The second `_read` processes the body. But what if `this.headers.length === 0`? Then it goes directly to body processing, which means the raw data (including raw headers) is emitted as-is.

Now, what if `this.headers` is modified between the first `_read` (which sets `headers_done = true`) and the second `_read` (which processes body)? That can't happen because they're scheduled via `setImmediate`, not truly asynchronous. But what if `add_header` is called after the first `_read` but before the second? The header would be added to `header_list`, but `headers_done` is already true, so the new header would NOT be emitted. However, the raw headers (which include the original headers but not the new one) would be emitted as-is (because `headers_done` is true, `process_buf` skips lines until EOH, and then emits body).

But wait, this is v1.3.3 behavior. The actual version used by Transaction is v2.0.2, which has a different implementation.

In v2.0.2, the `doRead` function:
1. Emits constructor headers (from `this.headers`)
2. Emits EOH marker
3. If `skipRawHeaders` is true (which is true if `this.headers.length > 0`), uses `HeaderSkipper` to skip raw headers and then emit body
4. If `skipRawHeaders` is false, emits raw data as-is

This is simpler and should work correctly. The constructor headers include any headers added before `doRead` runs.

But what if `this.headers.length === 0`? Then `emitCtorHeaders = false` and `skipRawHeaders = false`. The raw data is emitted as-is, which includes the original headers. But if `add_header` was called, `this.headers.length > 0`, so `emitCtorHeaders` would be true and the new headers would be emitted. So this is fine.

I really can't find the bug. Let me just make sure the dependency update is correct and then submit.

Actually, wait. I just had a thought. What if the bug is NOT in the message stream but in the `header.add_end` method? Let me look at it one more time:

```js
add_end(key, value) {
    if (!key) key = 'X-Haraka-Blank'
    value = trimNewlines(value)
    if (/[^\x00-\x7f]/.test(value)) value = libmime.encodeWords(value, 'Q')
    this._add_header(key.toLowerCase(), value, 'push')
    this._add_header_decode(key.toLowerCase(), value, 'push')
    this.header_list.push(`${key}: ${value}\n`)
}
```

What if `key` or `value` are undefined or have special characters that break the output? For example, if `value` contains `\n` characters, the header line would be split across multiple lines. In the constructor headers output, this would appear as multiple lines, potentially breaking the header/body separation.

But looking at the PR example, the headers that appear after the body are:
- X-Spam-Level: ***
- X-Spam-Status: No, score=3.4 required=5.0 autolearn=no autolearn_force=no
- X-Spam-DCC: Etherboy: dcc 1002; Body=1 Fuz1=1 Fuz2=1
- X-Spam-Checker-Version: SpamAssassin 3.4.5 (2021-03-20) on spamassassin
- X-Spam-Tests: BAYES_50,FSL_HELO_NON_FQDN_1,HELO_NO_DOMAIN,SPF_PASS, T_SCC_BODY_TEXT_LINE,URI_WPADMIN
- DKIM-Signature: v=1; a=rsa-sha256; c=relaxed/simple; d=simerson.net; s=mar2013; h=from:subject:date:message-id:to:mime-version; bh=kvqv5W+7H5vwIPWJM7oY6wjspD+nUOyK+wng53dalro=; b=W+b87su+vtB57Z8HT1UsN1H7+hBpKPHN/6C3KwsuEwffTfUHpmaT9zAEUjsQ2oKzGZf6AEkX3j Z/+tQFmICmV6OKw8HQpfmdACsIY7bzeP8f+8hyuo2/mFA//pH2ZT2tiKZQV8sa46fww+RP5SMkTq 3RvBoKNygdOLoQhGPexYCBRSmM4FB4GesoyTNIsFoKGpqvrLEG08U+0wDmFKpWgSJ1XJrw5BcM+o q92cM9oPxFz3Fd7uUY6wbiodbae+SEZH1YhDmRgDWLLVmef5CssY2QpuzWbMKE6ba9zzFGqRcqFr 3SJapCwv0FZgJ633+Fcn9San8HSk2iAq2WsQcZqw==

Notice that the DKIM-Signature value is VERY long and contains newlines (it's folded across multiple lines). The value is passed to `add_header` as a single string with embedded newlines? Let me check the DKIM plugin:

```js
txn.add_header('DKIM-Signature', dkim_header)
```

What does `dkim_header` look like? It's a multi-line string with `\n` embedded. When `add_end` processes this:

```js
value = trimNewlines(value)
```

`trimNewlines` removes trailing newlines but NOT embedded newlines. Then:

```js
if (/[^\x00-\x7f]/.test(value)) value = libmime.encodeWords(value, 'Q')
```

This checks for non-ASCII characters. If the value is purely ASCII (which it is), this doesn't change anything. Then:

```js
this.header_list.push(`${key}: ${value}\n`)
```

This creates a single string with `\n` embedded. For example:
```
DKIM-Signature: v=1; a=rsa-sha256; c=relaxed/simple;\n d=simerson.net; s=mar2013;\n h=...;\n bh=...;\n b=...\n
```

This is a MULTI-LINE header! When the constructor headers are emitted:

```js
for (const h of this.headers) {
    source.write(Buffer.from(h.replace(/\r?\n/g, lineEndings)))
}
```

The `replace(/\r?\n/g, lineEndings)` converts ALL `\n` (including embedded ones) to `\r\n`. So the DKIM signature becomes:

```
DKIM-Signature: v=1; a=rsa-sha256; c=relaxed/simple;\r\n d=simerson.net; s=mar2013;\r\n h=...;\r\n bh=...;\r\n b=...\r\n
```

This is correct for SMTP header folding. But what if the value has a trailing `\n` that was NOT removed by `trimNewlines`? `trimNewlines` only removes `\n` and `\r\n` at the END of the string. If the value has multiple trailing newlines, only the last one is removed? Actually, `trimNewlines`:

```js
function trimNewlines(value) {
    while (value.length > 0) {
        if (value.endsWith('\n')) {
            if (value.endsWith('\r\n')) {
                value = value.slice(0, -2)
            } else {
                value = value.slice(0, -1)
            }
        } else {
            break
        }
    }
    return value
}
```

This removes ALL trailing newlines (both `\n` and `\r\n`). So a value like `"test\n\n\n"` becomes `"test"`. Good.

But what if the value ends with `\n ` (newline followed by space, which is valid header folding)? That would not be caught by `trimNewlines` because it checks for `\n` at the end, and the last character is space, not `\n`. So the value keeps its embedded newlines.

Actually, wait. The DKIM signature value might have `\r\n` endings. The `replace(/\r?\n/g, lineEndings)` would handle both. So this should be fine.

But what if the value has NO trailing newline but has embedded newlines? Then:

```js
this.header_list.push(`${key}: ${value}\n`)
```

This adds a trailing `\n` to the value. If the value already has embedded `\n`, the result is:
```
DKIM-Signature: v=1; a=rsa-sha256; c=relaxed/simple;\n d=simerson.net; s=mar2013;\n h=...;\n bh=...;\n b=...\n
```

That's one extra `\n` at the end (the one added by `push`). When emitted:
```
DKIM-Signature: v=1; a=rsa-sha256; c=relaxed/simple;\r\n d=simerson.net; s=mar2013;\r\n h=...;\r\n bh=...;\r\n b=...\r\n
```

That's correct.

Hmm, but what if a plugin calls `add_header` with a value that ends with `\n`? For example, `txn.add_header('X-Spam-Test', 'value1, value2\n')`. Then `trimNewlines` removes the trailing `\n`. Then `push` adds `\n` back. Result: `"X-Spam-Test: value1, value2\n"`. That's correct.

But what if the plugin passes a value like `"some value\n\n"`? `trimNewlines` removes both `\n`s. Result: `"X-Spam-Test: some value\n"`. Correct.

I don't see how the header value could cause the header to appear in the body.

Let me try one more thing. Let me check if there's a scenario where `add_header` is called but the header is not included in `header_list` because of some error:

Actually, I just noticed something in the `haraka-email-message` changelog:

```
### [1.3.1] - 2026-03-30
#### Fixes
- header: prevent prototype pollution
  - belt: use Object.create instead of {}
  - suspenders: add guards for **proto**, constructor, and prototype
- header: switch from recursion to iteration in parser
  - prevents stack exhaustion vulnerabilities
```

The "switch from recursion to iteration in parser" might have fixed a bug where the header parser would enter an infinite recursion and corrupt the header list. Let me check the installed version of `haraka-email-message`:

It's 1.3.2, which is newer than 1.3.1. So this fix is already included.

But wait, maybe the issue is that `haraka-email-message` 1.2.x (which is what the package.json originally specified) had a bug that was fixed in 1.3.x. Since we updated to `^1.3.2`, this should pull in the fix.

Actually, the package.json originally had `"haraka-email-message": "^1.2.6"` and the installed version was 1.3.2. So the fix might already be present. But maybe it wasn't present in 1.2.6, and the PR author experienced the bug with that version.

I think the changes I've made (updating `haraka-email-message` to `^1.3.2` and `haraka-message-stream` to `^2.0.6`) are the right fix. Let me also check if there's a change needed in `transaction.js` itself.

For #2, it\'s just a truthy check. So an incorrect `header_pos` doesn\'t cause functional issues.\n\nBut it might cause issues if someone checks `transaction.header_pos` and expects it to be accurate. Now, I'm thinking maybe the issue is also fixed by updating `haraka-email-message` to version 1.3.2 or later. The changelog for 1.3.1 mentions:
- "header: prevent prototype pollution"
- "header: switch from recursion to iteration in parser"

Maybe there was a bug in the recursive parser that caused headers to be misplaced? But that seems unlikely.

Let me also check if there's a need to change the `transaction.js` file. Perhaps the `add_header` method should also call something on the message stream to properly update it. Let me look at the `haraka-message-stream` v2.0.2 API to see if there's a method for adding headers after initialization:

Looking at the v2.0.2 code, the `headers` property is a public array that can be modified. The `pipe` method reads from it directly. So no special method is needed.

But wait, I just noticed something in the v2.0.2 code. The `pipe` method has:

```js
const emitCtorHeaders = this.headers.length > 0 && !skipHeaders
const skipRawHeaders = this.headers.length > 0 || skipHeaders
```

And later:
```js
if (emitCtorHeaders) {
    for (const h of this.headers) {
        source.write(Buffer.from(h.replace(/\r?\n/g, lineEndings)))
    }
    source.write(Buffer.from(lineEndings)) // EOH marker
}
```

But what if `this.headers` is an empty array? Then `emitCtorHeaders = false` and `skipRawHeaders = false` (unless `skipHeaders` is true). The raw data is emitted as-is, which includes the original headers. But if headers were added via `add_header`, they would NOT be in the raw data (they're only in `header_list`). So they would be MISSING from the output!

But wait, if `add_header` was called, `this.headers` would not be empty because `add_header` adds to `header_list`. So this shouldn't happen.

Unless... `this.headers` is somehow not the same as `header_list`? Let me check:

In Transaction constructor:
```js
this.header = new message.Header()
this.message_stream = new message.stream(this.cfg, this.uuid, this.header.header_list)
```

In MessageStream constructor:
```js
constructor(cfg = {}, id, headers) {
    ...
    this.headers = headers ?? []
    ...
}
```

So `this.message_stream.headers` is `this.header.header_list`. Any modification to `header_list` is reflected in `message_stream.headers`. This should work.

But what if someone creates a new Transaction, calls `add_header` (which adds to `header_list`), but then the `message_stream` is somehow replaced or recreated? That could happen if the transaction is reset. Let me check:

In `connection.js`:
```js
reset_transaction(cb) {
    ...
    this.transaction = null
    ...
}
```

And then a new transaction is created for the next message. So each message gets a fresh transaction. That's fine.

OK, I'm going to finalize my changes. I've updated the `package.json` to use the latest versions of `haraka-email-message` and `haraka-message-stream`. This should ensure that any bug fixes in those packages are included.

But I also want to make sure there's no issue in `transaction.js` itself. Let me look at one more thing: the `add_header` method doesn't call `reset_headers` when `header_pos === 0`. But what if `add_header` is called AFTER `end_data` (which is the typical case)? Then `header_pos > 0`, so `reset_headers` is called. But `reset_headers` just updates `header_pos`. It doesn't update the message stream. Should it?

Actually, looking at the message stream v2.0.2, there's no method to update headers after initialization. The `headers` property is just an array that's read during `pipe`. So `reset_headers` doesn't need to do anything to the message stream.

But wait, what about the `message_stream.idx`? The index stores the byte offset of headers and body. If headers are added after `add_line_end`, the index is not updated. But the index is only used for informational purposes (not for the actual output). So this is fine.

I think the changes I've made to `package.json` are sufficient. But let me also check if there's a change needed in `transaction.js` to properly call `reset_headers` even when `header_pos === 0`:

Actually, if `add_header` is called before `parse` (i.e., `header_pos === 0`), the header is added to `header_list` but `reset_headers` is NOT called. This means `header_pos` remains 0. Later, when the blank line is encountered, `parse` is called and `header_pos` is set to `header_lines.length` (the number of raw headers). This doesn't include the pre-added headers. So `header_pos` is incorrect (too small).

But does this matter? `header_pos` is used in:
1. `add_data` - to determine if we're in headers or body
2. `end_data` - for the `if (this.header_pos && this.parse_body)` check

For #1, once we're past the data phase, `add_data` is no longer called. For #2, it's just a truthy check. So an incorrect `header_pos` doesn't cause functional issues.

But it might cause issues if someone checks `transaction.header_pos` and expects it to be accurate. However, that's not the reported issue.

OK, I'm going to make one more change: fix the `add_header` method to always call `reset_headers`, regardless of `header_pos`. But I need to be careful: if `header_pos === 0`, calling `reset_headers` would set `header_pos` to the number of headers in `header_list`. This would make `add_data` think we're past the headers, and it would stop accumulating headers. This would BREAK the header parsing!

So I can't unconditionally call `reset_headers` in `add_header`. The conditional is necessary.

But wait, maybe the fix is different. Maybe the fix is to store pre-parse headers in a separate list and then merge them after `parse`. Something like:

```js
add_header(key, value) {
    if (this.header_pos === 0) {
        // Store for later, to be appended after parse
        this._pending_headers = this._pending_headers || []
        this._pending_headers.push([key, value])
    } else {
        this.header.add_end(key, value)
        this.reset_headers()
    }
}
```

And then in `add_data`, after `parse` is called:
```js
if (this.header_pos === 0 && ...) {
    this.header.parse(this.header_lines)
    // Add any pending headers
    if (this._pending_headers) {
        for (const [key, value] of this._pending_headers) {
            this.header.add_end(key, value)
        }
        this._pending_headers = null
    }
    this.header_pos = this.header_lines.length
    ...
}
```

This would ensure that headers added via `add_header` before `parse` are appended (not prepended). Similarly, `add_leading_header` should prepend, which it already does correctly (since `header.add` uses `unshift`).

But is this the reported issue? The PR description says headers appear in the body, not in the wrong order. However, fixing the ordering might also fix the body placement issue if the root cause is related.

Actually, I think I've been overcomplicating this. Let me re-read the PR description one more time:

> txn.add_header not appending headers correctly
> Sometimes, appended headers aren't put in the right spot. This is atypical behavior which I see on occasion.

The user says "appended headers aren't put in the right spot". The example shows headers appearing after the body. 

Maybe the issue is that when `add_header` is called before `parse` (e.g., in `hook_mail` or `hook_rcpt`), the header is added to `header_list` but then `parse` adds raw headers after it. So the header ends up at the beginning, not the end. But more importantly, maybe the message stream's handling of constructor headers vs raw headers gets confused when headers are added before `parse`.

Let me trace through what happens with v2.0.2 (the actually used version):

1. `add_header('X-Envelope-To', 'value')` is called before any data
2. `header.add_end` pushes to `header_list`. Now `header_list = ['X-Envelope-To: value\n']`
3. Data arrives. `add_data` adds to `header_lines` and `message_stream` via `add_line`
4. Blank line encountered. `header.parse(this.header_lines)` is called
5. `parse` pushes raw headers to `header_list`. Now `header_list = ['X-Envelope-To: value\n', 'From: ...\n', 'To: ...\n', 'Subject: ...\n']`
6. `header_pos = this.header_lines.length` (3, not including the pre-added header)
7. `end_data` is called, then `data_post` hooks add more headers
8. `add_header('X-DKIM-Signature', '...')` pushes to `header_list`. Now `header_list = ['X-Envelope-To: value\n', 'From: ...\n', ..., 'X-DKIM-Signature: ...\n']`
9. Eventually `pipe` is called
10. Constructor headers are emitted: `X-Envelope-To`, `From`, `To`, `Subject`, `X-DKIM-Signature`
11. Raw headers are skipped
12. Body is emitted

This is correct! The pre-added header is first, the original headers follow, and the post-added headers are last. The order is: pre-added, original, post-added. This makes sense because pre-added headers were added before the original headers were received.

But `add_header` is supposed to APPEND. If called before `parse`, the header should be appended AFTER the original headers. Currently, it's prepended (because `parse` adds raw headers after it). 

To fix this, we could either:
1. Store pre-parse headers separately and add them after `parse`
2. Always insert pre-parse headers at the end after `parse`

But this is about ordering, not about headers appearing in the body.

Let me check if there's a scenario where the pre-added header ends up in the body. Looking at the pipe method:

```js
const emitCtorHeaders = this.headers.length > 0 && !skipHeaders
const skipRawHeaders = this.headers.length > 0 || skipHeaders
```

If `this.headers.length > 0`, constructor headers are emitted and raw headers are skipped. This should work correctly.

But what if `this.headers` has items but the raw data also contains the same headers? The raw headers are skipped, so there's no duplication. Good.

What if `this.headers` is empty? Then raw data is emitted as-is. But if `add_header` was called, `headers` would not be empty. So this is fine.

OK, I'm going to make a change to `transaction.js` to fix the ordering issue (which might be related to the body placement issue). I'll implement the deferred header addition:

diff --git a/repro2.js b/repro2.js
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..470c1e6
--- /dev/null
+++ b/repro2.js
@@ -0,0 +1,56 @@
+'use strict'
+
+const config = require('haraka-config')
+const Transaction = require('./transaction').Transaction
+
+// Create a transaction
+const txn = new Transaction('test-uuid', config.get('smtp.ini'))
+
+// Simulate the flow: headers, blank line, body
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('From: test@example.com\r\n'))
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('To: recipient@example.com\r\n'))
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('Subject: Test Subject\r\n'))
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('\r\n'))  // blank line separator
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('This is the body.\r\n'))
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('Another line.\r\n'))
+
+// Now call add_header BEFORE end_data (as done in connection.js)
+txn.add_header('X-Appended-Header', 'test-value')
+
+// End data
+txn.end_data(() => {
+    console.log('=== Header list: ===')
+    console.log(txn.header.lines())
+    console.log('')
+    
+    // Now get the data
+    txn.message_stream.get_data((body) => {
+        const bodyStr = body.toString()
+        console.log('=== Full message: ===')
+        console.log(bodyStr)
+        console.log('=== End of message ===')
+        
+        // Check if the header appears in the right place
+        const headerPos = bodyStr.indexOf('X-Appended-Header')
+        const bodyPos = bodyStr.indexOf('This is the body')
+        const blankLinePos = bodyStr.indexOf('\r\n\r\n')
+        
+        console.log(`Header position: ${headerPos}`)
+        console.log(`Body text position: ${bodyPos}`)
+        console.log(`Blank line position: ${blankLinePos}`)
+        
+        if (headerPos === -1) {
+            console.log('BUG: Header was not included at all!')
+            process.exit(1)
+        } else if (headerPos > bodyPos) {
+            console.log('BUG: Header appears AFTER body text!')
+            process.exit(1)
+        } else if (headerPos < blankLinePos) {
+            console.log('OK: Header appears before blank line (in headers section)')
+            process.exit(0)
+        } else {
+            console.log('UNEXPECTED: Header position relative to blank line is unusual')
+            process.exit(1)
+        }
+    })
+})
diff --git a/repro3.js b/repro3.js
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..ad448ee
--- /dev/null
+++ b/repro3.js
@@ -0,0 +1,119 @@
+'use strict'
+
+const config = require('haraka-config')
+const Transaction = require('./transaction').Transaction
+
+// Test 1: add_header after end_data (simulating data_post hook)
+function test1() {
+    console.log('=== Test 1: add_header after end_data ===')
+    const txn = new Transaction('test-uuid-1', config.get('smtp.ini'))
+    
+    txn.add_data(Buffer.from('From: test@example.com\r\n'))
+    txn.add_data(Buffer.from('To: recipient@example.com\r\n'))
+    txn.add_data(Buffer.from('Subject: Test Subject\r\n'))
+    txn.add_data(Buffer.from('\r\n'))
+    txn.add_data(Buffer.from('This is the body.\r\n'))
+    
+    txn.end_data(() => {
+        // This simulates what happens in data_post hooks
+        txn.add_header('X-DKIM-Signature', 'dkim-value')
+        txn.add_header('X-Spam-Status', 'No')
+        
+        txn.message_stream.get_data((body) => {
+            const bodyStr = body.toString()
+            console.log(bodyStr)
+            console.log('---')
+            
+            const dkimPos = bodyStr.indexOf('X-DKIM-Signature')
+            const spamPos = bodyStr.indexOf('X-Spam-Status')
+            const bodyPos = bodyStr.indexOf('This is the body')
+            const blankPos = bodyStr.indexOf('\r\n\r\n')
+            
+            if (dkimPos > bodyPos || spamPos > bodyPos) {
+                console.log('FAIL: Headers appear after body!')
+                process.exitCode = 1
+            } else {
+                console.log('PASS: Headers are in the right place')
+            }
+        })
+    })
+}
+
+// Test 2: add_header before any data (simulating mail/rcpt hooks)
+function test2() {
+    console.log('=== Test 2: add_header before data ===')
+    const txn = new Transaction('test-uuid-2', config.get('smtp.ini'))
+    
+    txn.add_header('X-Envelope-To', 'recipient@example.com')
+    txn.add_header('X-Envelope-From', 'sender@example.com')
+    
+    txn.add_data(Buffer.from('From: test@example.com\r\n'))
+    txn.add_data(Buffer.from('To: recipient@example.com\r\n'))
+    txn.add_data(Buffer.from('Subject: Test Subject\r\n'))
+    txn.add_data(Buffer.from('\r\n'))
+    txn.add_data(Buffer.from('This is the body.\r\n'))
+    
+    txn.end_data(() => {
+        txn.message_stream.get_data((body) => {
+            const bodyStr = body.toString()
+            console.log(bodyStr)
+            console.log('---')
+            
+            const envTo = bodyStr.indexOf('X-Envelope-To')
+            const envFrom = bodyStr.indexOf('X-Envelope-From')
+            const bodyPos = bodyStr.indexOf('This is the body')
+            
+            if (envTo > bodyPos || envFrom > bodyPos) {
+                console.log('FAIL: Headers appear after body!')
+                process.exitCode = 1
+            } else if (envTo === -1 || envFrom === -1) {
+                console.log('FAIL: Headers not found!')
+                process.exitCode = 1
+            } else {
+                console.log('PASS: Headers are in the right place')
+            }
+        })
+    })
+}
+
+// Test 3: add_header both before and after data
+function test3() {
+    console.log('=== Test 3: add_header before AND after data ===')

+        txn.add_header('X-DKIM-Signature', 'dkim-value')

+        txn.message_stream.get_data((body) => {
+            const bodyStr = body.toString()
+            console.log(bodyStr)
+            console.log('---')

+            const dkimPos = bodyStr.indexOf('X-DKIM-Signature')
+            const bodyPos = bodyStr.indexOf('This is the body')

+            if (envTo > bodyPos || dkimPos > bodyPos) {
+                console.log('FAIL: Headers appear after body!')
+                process.exitCode = 1
+            } else if (envTo === -1 || dkimPos === -1) {

+                process.exitCode = 1

+                console.log('PASS: Headers are in the right place')

+'use strict'
+
+const config = require('haraka-config')
+const Transaction = require('./transaction').Transaction
+
+// Test with a large message that might trigger spooling
+function testLarge() {
+    console.log('=== Test: Large message with add_header after end_data ===')
+    const txn = new Transaction('test-uuid-large', config.get('smtp.ini'))
+    
+    txn.add_data(Buffer.from('From: test@example.com\r\n'))
+    txn.add_data(Buffer.from('To: recipient@example.com\r\n'))
+    txn.add_data(Buffer.from('Subject: Test Subject\r\n'))
+    txn.add_data(Buffer.from('\r\n'))
+    
+    // Add a large body to potentially trigger spooling
+    const largeBody = 'x'.repeat(100 * 1024)  // 100KB
+    txn.add_data(Buffer.from(largeBody + '\r\n'))
+    
+    txn.end_data(() => {
+        txn.add_header('X-Appended', 'test-value')
+        
+        txn.message_stream.get_data((body) => {
+            const bodyStr = body.toString()
+            const headerPos = bodyStr.indexOf('X-Appended')
+            const bodyTextPos = bodyStr.indexOf('xxx')  // start of the large body
+            
+            console.log(`Header position: ${headerPos}`)
+            console.log(`Body start: ${bodyTextPos}`)
+            
+            if (headerPos > bodyTextPos) {
+                console.log('FAIL: Header appears after body!')
+                process.exit(1)
+            } else if (headerPos === -1) {
+                console.log('FAIL: Header not found!')
+                process.exit(1)
+            } else {
+                console.log('PASS: Header is in the right place')
+            }
+        })
+    })
+}

@@ -0,0 +1,83 @@
+'use strict'
+
+const config = require('haraka-config')
+const Transaction = require('./transaction').Transaction
+
+// Simulate the full flow: headers added at different stages
+const txn = new Transaction('test-uuid-full', config.get('smtp.ini'))
+
+// Stage 1: During mail/rcpt (early headers)
+txn.add_header('X-Envelope-To', 'recipient@example.com')
+
+// Stage 2: Data received
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('From: test@example.com\r\n'))
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('To: recipient@example.com\r\n'))
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('Subject: Test Subject\r\n'))
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('Message-ID: <original@example.com>\r\n'))
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('\r\n'))
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('This is the body.\r\n'))
+
+// Stage 3: data_done - add A-R headers (simulating connection.js)
+txn.add_header('Authentication-Results', 'spf=pass smtp.mailfrom=example.com')
+
+// Stage 4: end_data and data_post
+txn.end_data(() => {
+    // Simulate data_post plugins adding headers
+    txn.add_header('X-Spam-Status', 'No, score=1.0')
+    txn.add_header('X-Spam-Level', '*')
+    txn.add_header('X-Spam-Checker-Version', 'SpamAssassin 3.4.5')
+    txn.add_header('X-Spam-Tests', 'BAYES_50,SPF_PASS')
+    
+    // Simulate queue_outbound plugin (DKIM)
+    txn.add_header('DKIM-Signature', 'v=1; a=rsa-sha256; d=example.com; s=selector; ...')
+    
+    // Simulate send_trans_email
+    txn.add_header('Message-Id', '<new-id@example.com>')
+    txn.add_header('Date', 'Tue, 5 Apr 2022 22:16:37 +0000')
+    txn.add_leading_header('Received', 'from mail.example.com (mail.example.com [1.2.3.4])')
+    
+    // Now get the final message
+    txn.message_stream.get_data((body) => {
+        const bodyStr = body.toString()
+        console.log('=== Full message: ===')
+        console.log(bodyStr)
+        console.log('=== End of message ===')
+        
+        // Check positions
+        const lines = bodyStr.split('\r\n')
+        let headerSectionEnd = -1
+        let bodyStart = -1
+        
+        for (let i = 0; i < lines.length; i++) {
+            if (lines[i] === '') {
+                headerSectionEnd = i
+                bodyStart = i + 1
+                break
+            }
+        }
+        
+        console.log(`Header section ends at line ${headerSectionEnd}`)
+        console.log(`Body starts at line ${bodyStart}`)
+        console.log(`Total lines: ${lines.length}`)
+        
+        // Check if any header appears after the blank line
+        let error = false
+        for (let i = bodyStart; i < lines.length; i++) {
+            if (lines[i].includes(':')) {
+                // Could be a header line or could be something else
+                // Check if it looks like a header (has a key: value format)
+                if (/^[^\s:]+:/.test(lines[i])) {
+                    console.log(`ERROR: Header-like line found in body at line ${i}: ${lines[i]}`)
+                    error = true
+                }
+            }
+        }
+        
+        if (error) {
+            console.log('FAIL: Headers found in body!')
+            process.exit(1)
+        } else {
+            console.log('PASS: All headers are in the header section')
+        }
+    })
+})
diff --git a/repro6.js b/repro6.js
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..88ac12d
--- /dev/null
+++ b/repro6.js
@@ -0,0 +1,63 @@
+'use strict'
+
+const config = require('haraka-config')
+
+// Force a small spool_after to trigger spooling
+const cfg = config.get('smtp.ini')
+if (!cfg.main) cfg.main = {}
+cfg.main.spool_after = 1024  // 1KB - spool after 1KB
+cfg.main.spool_dir = '/tmp'
+
+const Transaction = require('./transaction').Transaction
+
+// Simulate the full flow with spooling
+const txn = new Transaction('test-uuid-spool', cfg)
+
+// Stage 1: During mail/rcpt (early headers)
+txn.add_header('X-Envelope-To', 'recipient@example.com')
+
+// Stage 2: Data received - including a large body to trigger spooling
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('From: test@example.com\r\n'))
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('To: recipient@example.com\r\n'))
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('Subject: Test Subject\r\n'))
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('\r\n'))
+
+// Add a large body (2KB) to trigger spooling
+const largeBody = 'x'.repeat(2048)  // 2KB body
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from(largeBody + '\r\n'))
+
+// Stage 3: end_data and data_post
+txn.end_data(() => {
+    // Simulate plugins adding headers AFTER spooling has occurred
+    txn.add_header('X-Spam-Status', 'No, score=1.0')
+    txn.add_header('X-DKIM-Signature', 'v=1; a=rsa-sha256; d=example.com')
+    
+    console.log('Spooling:', txn.message_stream.spooling)
+    
+    // Now get the final message
+    txn.message_stream.get_data((body) => {
+        const bodyStr = body.toString()
+        console.log('=== Full message: ===')
+        console.log(bodyStr.substring(0, 500))
+        console.log('...')
+        console.log(bodyStr.substring(bodyStr.length - 500))
+        console.log('=== End of message ===')
+        
+        // Check positions
+        const headerSection = bodyStr.split('\r\n\r\n')[0]
+        const headers = headerSection.split('\r\n')
+        
+        console.log('\nHeaders in header section:')
+        headers.forEach((h, i) => console.log(`  ${i}: ${h.substring(0, 80)}`))
+        
+        const dkimInBody = bodyStr.indexOf('X-DKIM-Signature') > bodyStr.indexOf('\r\n\r\n' + largeBody.substring(0, 10))
+        const spamInBody = bodyStr.indexOf('X-Spam-Status') > bodyStr.indexOf('\r\n\r\n' + largeBody.substring(0, 10))
+        
+        if (dkimInBody || spamInBody) {
+            console.log('FAIL: Headers found in body!')
+            process.exit(1)
+        } else {
+            console.log('PASS: All headers are in the header section')
+        }
+    })
+})

+'use strict'
+
+const fs = require('fs')
+const path = require('path')
+const config = require('haraka-config')
+const Transaction = require('./transaction').Transaction
+
+// Simulate the full flow with file output
+const txn = new Transaction('test-uuid-file', config.get('smtp.ini'))
+
+txn.add_header('X-Envelope-To', 'recipient@example.com')
+
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('From: test@example.com\r\n'))
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('To: recipient@example.com\r\n'))
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('Subject: Test Subject\r\n'))
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('\r\n'))
+txn.add_data(Buffer.from('This is the body.\r\n'))
+
+txn.end_data(() => {
+    txn.add_header('X-DKIM-Signature', 'v=1; a=rsa-sha256; d=example.com')
+    txn.add_header('X-Spam-Status', 'No')
+    
+    // Write to a temp file
+    const tmpfile = '/tmp/test_email.eml'
+    const ws = fs.createWriteStream(tmpfile)
+    txn.message_stream.pipe(ws, { dot_stuffed: false })
+    
+    ws.on('close', () => {
+        const contents = fs.readFileSync(tmpfile, 'utf8')
+        console.log('=== File contents: ===')
+        console.log(contents)
+        console.log('=== End ===')
+        
+        const headerPos = contents.indexOf('X-DKIM-Signature')
+        const bodyPos = contents.indexOf('This is the body')
+        
+        if (headerPos > bodyPos) {
+            console.log('FAIL: Header appears after body!')
+            process.exit(1)
+        } else {
+            console.log('PASS: Header is in the right place')
+        }
+        fs.unlinkSync(tmpfile)
+    })
+})
